analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Carlos Prates -- Style & Profile

- Prates is a switch-stance pressure striker who walks people down and punishes them with powerful combinations. He has finished 8 of his last 9 fights by KO/TKO — this is not a coincidence, it is a pattern. He finds the finish.
- His 5-inch reach advantage over Della Maddalena is a real structural asset. He can initiate exchanges from a range where his opponent can't comfortably respond, and his switch-stance gives him an additional layer of unpredictability on the lead hand.
- His career striking numbers (4.41 landed/min at 54% accuracy) suggest a fighter who is accurate without being reckless. The last three fights show a wide SLpM variance — 6.42 against Neal, 1.66 against Della Maddalena in the rematch — which actually signals adaptability: he can brawl when needed or fight methodically when the moment calls for it.
- His defensive numbers are the soft spot. A 47% strike defense rate is below average for a UFC main-eventer, and he absorbs 4.30 significant strikes per minute at career average — nearly as much as he lands. He wins these fights because his output hurts people first, not because he avoids getting hurt.
- He does not wrestle. Zero takedown attempts across his recent UFC run, and he does not threaten submissions. This is entirely a striking contest on his terms, which simplifies the matchup considerably.
- Coming into this fight, Prates is on a three-fight KO streak over genuinely ranked opposition — Neal, Edwards (the then-champion), and Della Maddalena in the first meeting. He is operating at the peak of his UFC run.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Jack Della Maddalena -- Style & Profile

- No UFC fight history exists to draw per-minute statistics from. Everything here is derived from pre-UFC record and the known result of the first Prates fight, which Prates won by KO in Round 3. That result is itself a data point worth taking seriously.
- His 18-4 record across pre-UFC competition suggests a fighter with legitimate credentials, but regional and international promotion opponents vary wildly in quality. Without specific UFC-level stat splits, the confidence on any projection is limited.
- He was stopped by Prates in three rounds in their first meeting. The manner of that loss — a KO/TKO in the third round — raises a question about whether he carries accumulated damage into a rematch against the same opponent who already broke him down and finished him.
- Coming into what is described as a UFC debut context (the data shows no UFC fight history parsed), Della Maddalena faces the standard adjustment challenges: glove size, cage dimensions, opponent quality jump. But he also carries the psychological weight of already knowing what Prates' power feels like.
- At 5'11" with a 73-inch reach against a 6'1" opponent with 78 inches, he is at a persistent range disadvantage. To win this fight, he has to close distance consistently and work inside, where Prates' reach advantage is neutralized. That is difficult against a pressure fighter who initiates exchanges at his own preferred range.
- Without UFC strike defense or absorption numbers, the durability picture is incomplete. What we do know is he was finished once already by this specific opponent, which is the most contextually relevant data point available.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This is a rematch, which means the most important analytical input is the first fight: Prates won by KO in Round 3 of a 5-round scheduled bout. He did not need the full distance. Della Maddalena survived into the third round, which at minimum confirms he has some durability, but the finish suggests Prates eventually found the timing or the damage accumulated enough that the stoppage came. In a rematch, the fighter who won the first meeting retains a meaningful psychological and tactical advantage — he knows he can hurt the other man, and the man who was stopped knows it too.

Structurally, the 5-inch reach edge for Prates is the defining physical variable. Della Maddalena has to fight at a range that is uncomfortable for him just to enter exchanges, and Prates is an active, forward-pressure fighter who will not passively allow him to get comfortable. Prates' switch stance compounds this problem — when you are already fighting from a range deficit, the inability to predict the lead hand makes the distance management even harder. Della Maddalena's path to victory runs through sustained inside work: if he can negate the reach by staying chest-to-chest, shortening combinations, and making the fight ugly, he has a chance. But that requires solving a problem he did not solve the first time.

The net intangible picture tilts heavily toward Prates. He is on a three-fight KO streak over quality opposition, has the physical advantages, already has a finish over this specific opponent, and is fighting at altitude that neither man will feel much given the venue sits at sea level. Della Maddalena's only meaningful edge would be if the first fight generated genuine tactical learning — camps sometimes use a loss as a complete rebuild rather than a minor adjustment. Without visibility into what changed in preparation, that possibility has to be treated as speculative rather than evidential.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 — Carlos Prates wins by KO/TKO, Rounds 2–3**
Likelihood: High
Why: Prates finished Della Maddalena in Round 3 in their first meeting, and rematches against knockout finishers rarely go better for the man who was already stopped. Prates' reach advantage will create the same distance management problems, and the accumulated pressure of a 5-round fight typically exposes the durability question that already surfaced once. Rounds 2–3 is the expected window based on the first fight's pacing and Prates' tendency to finish opponents after wearing them down rather than in the first minute.

**#2 — Carlos Prates wins by KO/TKO, Round 1**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Prates has finished opponents in Round 1 three times in his recent run (Neal, Magny, Radtke), and a fighter returning from a KO loss sometimes enters a rematch with tightened-up defensive habits that actually create openings for a well-timed early shot. If Della Maddalena overadjusts or gets caught early by the reach disadvantage, an earlier finish is genuinely in play.

**#3 — Jack Della Maddalena wins by KO/TKO, Rounds 2–4**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Prates absorbs 4.30 significant strikes per minute and defends only 47% of incoming shots — he is hittable. If Della Maddalena spent the camp specifically solving the inside-distance problem and arrives with sharper combination work at close range, there is a legitimate path to hurting Prates. This is the realistic scenario for an upset, not a one-shot early crack, but sustained inside work that catches Prates in a moment where his own offense has him squared up.

**#4 — Carlos Prates wins by unanimous decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Prates has zero decision wins in his recent run and appears to neither seek nor benefit from late-fight chess matches. If Della Maddalena's camp has made him genuinely difficult to finish — better head movement, tighter guard, constant cage control — this fight could reach the scorecards. But Prates would likely win on volume and reach across five rounds even without a finish. A low-probability scenario but not impossible.
analysis_sections.betting:
**Tier: PLAY**
**Primary Pick: Carlos Prates**
Confidence: 68%

The line movement here is worth understanding before placing anything. Prates opened at -175 and has drifted to -120 — a significant lengthening of roughly 55 cents. Della Maddalena opened near pick-em at -104 and has moved to +150. This is not reverse line movement in the traditional sense (RLM would require the line moving against the public-bet direction), but rather a dramatic line swing suggesting sharp or informed money has moved substantially onto Della Maddalena. That is a real signal and cannot be ignored.

However, this is also the cleanest possible "market exception" scenario from a structural standpoint: it is a rematch where one fighter has already finished the other, there is a 5-inch reach advantage in play, the winner has been on a three-fight KO streak over ranked opposition, and the loser has no visible UFC strike statistics to anchor any alternative read on. The most likely explanation for the line move is that sharp bettors believe the underdog's camp has made significant adjustments — a legitimate bet when you have no insight into what those adjustments are, but not necessarily a reason to abandon the structural read entirely.

Taking Prates at -120 rather than -175 is objectively a better price on the same fighter, and the structural case remains intact: he has the reach, the power, the recent form, and the existing finish over this specific opponent.

**Line:** Carlos Prates -120
**Implied probability (raw):** 54.5%
**De-vigged true probability (using current -120 / +150 market):** Prates 54.5%, Della Maddalena 40.0% — sum 94.5%, meaning de-vigged Prates sits at approximately 57.7%
**Assessed probability:** 65%
**Stake:** 1.5 units
**Target line:** The bet holds value down to approximately -155. At current -120, there is meaningful room before the value evaporates.

**Data context:** Prates' read is built on solid UFC fight history with a relevant recent sample. Della Maddalena's read is built almost entirely on pre-UFC data and the first-fight result — that creates genuine uncertainty, which is reflected in the stake sizing rather than the pick direction.

**Active consideration:** The dramatic line move toward Della Maddalena is the one factor that should make any bettor pause. If you have reason to believe the camp adjustment story is real — specific news about training, sparring, or tactical overhaul — the case for passing or taking the other side strengthens. Without that information, the structural read on Prates holds.

**Hedge verdict: MARGINAL ARB**
With Prates at -120 and Della Maddalena at +150, the combined implied probability is approximately 94.5% — just below 100%, creating a marginal arbitrage opportunity.

For a $100 total stake:
- Decimal odds: Prates 1.833 (−120), Della Maddalena 2.50 (+150)
- Stake on Prates = $100 × (2.50 / (1.833 + 2.50)) = $100 × 0.577 = $57.70
- Stake on Della Maddalena = $100 × (1.833 / 4.333) = $100 × 0.423 = $42.30
- Payout if Prates wins: $57.70 × 1.833 = $105.76
- Payout if Della Maddalena wins: $42.30 × 2.50 = $105.75
- Guaranteed profit: ~$5.75 on $100 → approximately 5.75% ROI

This is a genuine, small arb. If you can access best lines on both sides across different books, the hedge is mathematically profitable regardless of outcome. The margin is slim enough that line movement between the time you read this and placement could erase it — move quickly or not at all. Note that frequent hedging across the same book pair can draw account scrutiny over time.

**Props:** No prop value identified. Without Della Maddalena's UFC defensive statistics, a "fight goes to decision" or round-specific prop cannot be reliably de-vigged from the underlying finish probability math. The most defensible structural play remains the Prates moneyline.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Quillan Salkilld -- Style & Profile

- Salkilld is a finishing machine who works at a high pace early and puts fights away quickly — all three of his most recent wins came in Round 1, two by KO and one by submission. He hasn't been taken past the first round in over a year.
- His striking accuracy sits at a genuine 57% career average, and against Haqparast he landed nearly six significant strikes per minute. He's not just busy — he's precise when he commits.
- The wrestling is elite. Career takedown average of 7.25 per 15 minutes is an extraordinary number, and his 80% takedown defense means opponents almost never get to use grappling against him on their terms.
- Against Mullarkey he showed a different dimension — nearly 10 takedowns per 15 minutes and a submission finish. He can dictate where the fight happens and finish either way.
- At 26 with a 12-1 record and three straight first-round finishes against legitimate lightweight opposition, Salkilld is operating with real momentum entering this rematch.
- His one career loss is the only blemish on a record that otherwise shows a fighter who imposes his will from the opening bell and doesn't let opponents settle.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Beneil Dariush -- Style & Profile

- Dariush has been knocked out in four of his last five fights, and three of those stoppages came in Round 1. That's not a trend anymore — it's a pattern that defines where he is in his career at 36.
- His last three fights tell the story clearly: an output of 0.86 significant strikes per minute against Salkilld, 11.25 against Saint Denis (a wild brawl he lost in 16 seconds), and 1.47 against Moicano. The consistency is gone; the volume is unpredictable and the results have been brutal.
- The Benoit Saint Denis loss is particularly alarming — he was stopped in 16 seconds, which isn't a chess match going wrong, it's a chin that can no longer absorb elite-level power without catastrophic consequences.
- Career strike defense of 57% is respectable on paper, but over his last three fights that number is effectively irrelevant — he's being hit clean by opponents with real power and going down fast.
- He does have two historical weight misses on record, which adds a layer of concern about his body's relationship with the 155-pound limit, though it hasn't been a headline issue recently.
- His best version — the wrestler-decision fighter who beat Gamrot and Ferguson — relies on grinding, controlling pace, and surviving rounds. That version of Dariush has not shown up since 2022, and at 36, the physical tools that enabled it are declining.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The most important thing to understand about this rematch is that the first fight already happened — Salkilld knocked Dariush out in Round 1 three and a half minutes into a fight that wasn't particularly close before the finish. That result didn't happen in a vacuum. Dariush has now been finished by strikes four times in his last five outings, three of them before the end of the first round. Salkilld is a pressure striker with genuine power and a finishing instinct that activates early. The matchup structure strongly favors the younger, faster, harder-hitting fighter — and that is unambiguously Salkilld.

Dariush's realistic path to winning runs through a style he hasn't deployed effectively in years: wrestling-heavy, clinch-control, pace-neutralizing grappling. Against Salkilld's 80% takedown defense, even attempting that game plan comes with a low success ceiling. In the first fight, Dariush managed 8.61 takedowns per 15 minutes in terms of rate — but the fight lasted only 3 minutes and 29 seconds, and he still lost by knockout. The numbers from that fight show Dariush was active on the ground in brief stretches but couldn't prevent the striking exchanges that ended the night. His 3-inch reach disadvantage also means he cannot comfortably operate at range, and Salkilld's footwork and finishing instinct make staying on the outside a dangerous long-term strategy.

The net picture here is stark: Salkilld is younger, faster, more accurate, hits harder, and has already finished this exact opponent. Dariush would need to either significantly out-wrestle a man with elite takedown defense — unlikely — or survive long enough for Salkilld to gas out, which has never happened in Salkilld's recent fights. At 36 with accumulated stoppage damage and a cracked chin, the probability of Dariush pulling off a reversal in a rematch is very low. The line moving from -350 to -450 on Salkilld reflects the market reaching the same conclusion.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Salkilld wins by KO/TKO, Round 1**
Likelihood: High
Why: This is exactly how the first fight ended, and nothing about Dariush's trajectory since then suggests the result would be different. Salkilld lands clean early, Dariush's chin cannot absorb elite power at 36, and the fight ends before it gets to the second round. Three of Dariush's last five losses ended in Round 1 by strikes.

**#2 -- Salkilld wins by KO/TKO, Round 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: If Dariush manages to survive round one through activity, clinch work, or early wrestling, Salkilld's pace and accuracy eventually find a home. Dariush's cardio at 36 means his defensive output will drop, and Salkilld has shown the ability to stay sharp into later rounds.

**#3 -- Salkilld wins by submission, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Against Mullarkey, Salkilld showed willingness to drag a fight to the mat and finish by choke when the opportunity presents itself. If Dariush shoots for a takedown and Salkilld gets the reversal or back, a submission finish is within Salkilld's toolkit.

**#4 -- Dariush wins by decision**
Likelihood: Very Low
Why: The only scenario where Dariush wins is a near-complete tactical reset — elite wrestling, zero striking exchanges, and Salkilld being stifled for 15 minutes. His best version of that game plan beat Ferguson and Gamrot in 2021-22. Against Salkilld's takedown defense and pressure, replicating it seems implausible, but it cannot be entirely ruled out as a theoretical floor.
analysis_sections.betting:
**Tier: NO BET on Salkilld**

Salkilld is almost certainly winning this fight. The first result, the trajectory, the age gap, the chin damage on Dariush — everything points the same direction. But the line has moved to -450, and at those odds, a $450 risk returns only $100. The implied probability after removing the book's margin sits around 81-82%. Even with a very high assessed win probability for Salkilld — call it 83-85% — the gap between what the market is offering and what the data supports is inside the noise. There is no meaningful value on the moneyline at this price.

**Primary Pick: PASS**

The line opened at -350 and has shortened to -450 on Salkilld. The movement is one-directional and makes logical sense given the rematch context — this is the market correctly re-evaluating a fight where one fighter knocked the other out already. There is no reverse line movement here, no sharp signal pointing the other way. The books have priced this accurately, possibly generously for themselves.

**Market data:** Salkilld opened -350, now -450. Dariush opened +285 (noting this appears to be the current Dariush line — his opening was around +285 equivalent to Salkilld -350). Movement is entirely toward Salkilld, which is logical, not sharp-money-driven.

**Stake:** No bet — value is absent at current price.

**Target line:** Salkilld would represent a playable value around -250 or shorter. At -450, the math does not clear the minimum edge threshold regardless of how confident the directional read is.

**Hedge verdict: NO ARB.** At -450 / approximately +285 on Dariush, the combined implied probability is approximately 81.8% + 25.9% = 107.7%. No arbitrage exists. Do not hedge.

**Props worth considering:** A "fight ends in Round 1" prop for Salkilld is worth examining if available at reasonable odds. Three of Salkilld's last three wins, including the first meeting with Dariush, ended in Round 1. Three of Dariush's last five losses also ended in Round 1. If a Round 1 finish prop is available at +100 or better, the math supports it — the base rate from both fighters' recent histories is meaningfully above 50%. De-vig before committing, and verify the prop line is competitive.

Both fighters have layoff times listed as 1 day, which suggests this is the rematch data being input on fight day — no ring rust concerns apply to either fighter.

- Career strike defense of 57% is respectable on paper, but over his last three fights that number is effectively irrelevant — he's being hit clean by opponents with real power and going down fast.
- He does have two historical weight misses on record, which adds a layer of concern about his body's relationship with the 155-pound limit, though it hasn't been a headline issue recently.
- His best version — the wrestler-decision fighter who beat Gamrot and Ferguson — relies on grinding, controlling pace, and surviving rounds. That version of Dariush has not shown up since 2022, and at 36, the physical tools that enabled it are declining.
<!--END-->

<!--HEAD2HEAD-->
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The most important thing to understand about this rematch is that the first fight already happened — Salkilld knocked Dariush out in Round 1 three and a half minutes into a fight that wasn't particularly close before the finish. That result didn't happen in a vacuum. Dariush has now been finished by strikes four times in his last five outings, three of them before the end of the first round. Salkilld is a pressure striker with genuine power and a finishing instinct that activates early. The matchup structure strongly favors the younger, faster, harder-hitting fighter — and that is unambiguously Salkilld.

Dariush's realistic path to winning runs through a style he hasn't deployed effectively in years: wrestling-heavy, clinch-control, pace-neutralizing grappling. Against Salkilld's 80% takedown defense, even attempting that game plan comes with a low success ceiling. In the first fight, Dariush managed 8.61 takedowns per 15 minutes in terms of rate — but the fight lasted only 3 minutes and 29 seconds, and he still lost by knockout. The numbers from that fight show Dariush was active on the ground in brief stretches but couldn't prevent the striking exchanges that ended the night. His 3-inch reach disadvantage also means he cannot comfortably operate at range, and Salkilld's footwork and finishing instinct make staying on the outside a dangerous long-term strategy.

The net picture here is stark: Salkilld is younger, faster, more accurate, hits harder, and has already finished this exact opponent. Dariush would need to either significantly out-wrestle a man with elite takedown defense — unlikely — or survive long enough for Salkilld to gas out, which has never happened in Salkilld's recent fights. At 36 with accumulated stoppage damage and a cracked chin, the probability of Dariush pulling off a reversal in a rematch is very low. The line moving from -350 to -450 on Salkilld reflects the market reaching the same conclusion.

<!--END-->

<!--ENDINGS-->
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Salkilld wins by KO/TKO, Round 1**
Likelihood: High
Why: This is exactly how the first fight ended, and nothing about Dariush's trajectory since then suggests the result would be different. Salkilld lands clean early, Dariush's chin cannot absorb elite power at 36, and the fight ends before it gets to the second round. Three of Dariush's last five losses ended in Round 1 by strikes.

**#2 -- Salkilld wins by KO/TKO, Round 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: If Dariush manages to survive round one through activity, clinch work, or early wrestling, Salkilld's pace and accuracy eventually find a home. Dariush's cardio at 36 means his defensive output will drop, and Salkilld has shown the ability to stay sharp into later rounds.

**#3 -- Salkilld wins by submission, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Against Mullarkey, Salkilld showed willingness to drag a fight to the mat and finish by choke when the opportunity presents itself. If Dariush shoots for a takedown and Salkilld gets the reversal or back, a submission finish is within Salkilld's toolkit.

**#4 -- Dariush wins by decision**
Likelihood: Very Low
Why: The only scenario where Dariush wins is a near-complete tactical reset — elite wrestling, zero striking exchanges, and Salkilld being stifled for 15 minutes. His best version of that game plan beat Ferguson and Gamrot in 2021-22. Against Salkilld's takedown defense and pressure, replicating it seems implausible, but it cannot be entirely ruled out as a theoretical floor.

<!--END-->

<!--BETTING-->
**Tier: NO BET on Salkilld**

Salkilld is almost certainly winning this fight. The first result, the trajectory, the age gap, the chin damage on Dariush — everything points the same direction. But the line has moved to -450, and at those odds, a $450 risk returns only $100. The implied probability after removing the book's margin sits around 81-82%. Even with a very high assessed win probability for Salkilld — call it 83-85% — the gap between what the market is offering and what the data supports is inside the noise. There is no meaningful value on the moneyline at this price.

**Primary Pick: PASS**

The line opened at -350 and has shortened to -450 on Salkilld. The movement is one-directional and makes logical sense given the rematch context — this is the market correctly re-evaluating a fight where one fighter knocked the other out already. There is no reverse line movement here, no sharp signal pointing the other way. The books have priced this accurately, possibly generously for themselves.

**Market data:** Salkilld opened -350, now -450. Dariush opened +285 (noting this appears to be the current Dariush line — his opening was around +285 equivalent to Salkilld -350). Movement is entirely toward Salkilld, which is logical, not sharp-money-driven.

**Stake:** No bet — value is absent at current price.

**Target line:** Salkilld would represent a playable value around -250 or shorter. At -450, the math does not clear the minimum edge threshold regardless of how confident the directional read is.

**Hedge verdict: NO ARB.** At -450 / approximately +285 on Dariush, the combined implied probability is approximately 81.8% + 25.9% = 107.7%. No arbitrage exists. Do not hedge.

**Props worth considering:** A "fight ends in Round 1" prop for Salkilld is worth examining if available at reasonable odds. Three of Salkilld's last three wins, including the first meeting with Dariush, ended in Round 1. Three of Dariush's last five losses also ended in Round 1. If a Round 1 finish prop is available at +100 or better, the math supports it — the base rate from both fighters' recent histories is meaningfully above 50%. De-vig before committing, and verify the prop line is competitive.

Both fighters have layoff times listed as 1 day, which suggests this is the rematch data being input on fight day — no ring rust concerns apply to either fighter.
<!--END-->

## Steve Erceg -- Style & Profile

- Erceg fights as a pressure-forward striker who wants to dictate the pace and center of the cage, but his last three fights reveal a fighter who has significantly throttled back on volume — he's averaging just 1.6, 0.2, and 1.0 significant strikes per minute across those outings, well below his career norm of 4.64. That's not a striker pressing his advantages; that's a fighter surviving.
- His striking accuracy has dipped into the high-20s and mid-30s recently, which suggests either a deliberate gameplan shift toward control-based winning or a timing/confidence issue that emerged after back-to-back title-fight losses to Pantoja and Moreno. Either way, the output has cratered.
- On the feet, his 55% strike defense and 67% takedown defense are serviceable but not elite — he can be hit and he can be taken down. Against a live grappler, those numbers matter.
- His one KO win (Schnell, Round 2, 26 seconds) shows genuine power exists in his hands, but his finishing rate over the last eight fights is almost entirely decisions — four of five wins went the distance.
- At 30, he is firmly on the right side of the age curve, and a 2-inch reach advantage over Elliott is real but not fight-altering at flyweight. His best path to winning is through activity and cage control, not power.
- Worth noting: his layoff reads as 1 day, which is a data artifact — this fight against Elliott already appears in his record as a win by unanimous decision on May 2, 2026. The analysis here is being run on a rematch context where Erceg already has a blueprint on Elliott from a recent win.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Tim Elliott -- Style & Profile

- Elliott is a chaos-seeking, high-motor grappler who has built his late-career identity around grinding out takedowns, scrambling into submissions, and overwhelming opponents with relentless activity. His 3.61 takedowns per 15 minutes and two submission wins in his last three active fights reflect a man who found a formula that still works at 39.
- The grappling is real, but the striking has always been secondary — 43% accuracy and 3.51 SLpM career numbers that have dropped significantly in recent fights (2.6 against Erceg, 1.66 against Asakura). He's not winning standing up, and he knows it.
- His takedown defense at 59% is a genuine liability. Opponents who want to out-wrestle him or neutralize his offense by stuffing his shots have a clear opening — over one in three of his takedown attempts get stopped, which means he needs volume shooting to make the math work.
- He has two weight misses in his fight history, which at 39 is a meaningful signal. His body has been at flyweight for a long time and the cuts are not getting easier. There's no specific recent miss confirmed here, but the pattern exists.
- At 39, Elliott is past the age cliff for men's flyweight. His chin has never been dramatically cracked — he's been submitted more than knocked out — but his pace and recovery are legitimate concerns in the later rounds of any fight. His last three performances have all been decided on the scorecards or by submission, with no late-round KO losses, so the durability concern is more about cardio than chin integrity.
- He already lost to Erceg once by decision in their first meeting. Coming back on short turnaround, in Erceg's home region, at 39 years old, asking for a different result requires a specific adjustment that we haven't seen evidence of.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The first fight already answered most of the structural questions here. Erceg beat Elliott by unanimous decision, and the stylistic reason is visible in the numbers: when Erceg commits to a defensive, control-oriented gameplan, Elliott's grappling offense gets neutralized by a fighter who is longer, younger, and more composed in the pocket. Elliott's 59% takedown defense means he gives up shots, but his 45% takedown accuracy also means he misses more than he lands — and against a patient, disciplined Erceg, those missed shots become the story of the fight.

Erceg's striking volume has been unusually low recently, which is the one argument in Elliott's favor. If Erceg shows up again with the same muted 1–2 SLpM output, Elliott can stay competitive on volume and scramble frequency alone. But Erceg's strike defense (55%) and the reach advantage give him enough of a buffer to keep exchanges clean even when he's not throwing much. The real edge Erceg holds is pace management — he's nine years younger and has shown he can impose a tempo Elliott cannot sustain for three rounds. Elliott's late-round output has trended down across recent fights, and a younger, fresher opponent who forces action is exactly the kind of matchup that exposes that.

The intangible picture tilts heavily against Elliott. He is 39, already lost this fight once, has a history of weight cut issues, and is fighting in Australia — Erceg's home territory — with a fan base that may influence a close scorecard. His path to winning requires either a submission finish (which demands getting the fight down and staying there against a 67% TD-defending opponent) or a more active, punch-output version of himself that we have not seen in recent outings. Erceg simply needs to replicate what he already did, with the added comfort of having done it before.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Steve Erceg wins by unanimous decision**
Likelihood: High
Why: This is the most probable outcome because it already happened. Erceg controls pace, uses his youth and reach to limit Elliott's wrestling entries, and wins on activity and cage generalship across three rounds. Elliott's submission threat requires getting the fight to the mat and staying there, which Erceg's 67% TD defense makes difficult to sustain.

**#2 -- Tim Elliott wins by submission**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Elliott's two recent submission wins (Asakura, Sumudaerji) show the finishing threat is alive, and if he can force a scramble or capitalize on a mistake from Erceg, his experience off his back or in top control could produce a finish. However, Erceg has not been submitted in his UFC run and is not a fighter who gifts scramble positions cheaply.

**#3 -- Steve Erceg wins by KO/TKO**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Erceg has the power — his finish of Schnell in 26 seconds is genuine evidence. Elliott's chin has held up reasonably well historically, and Erceg's recent striking volume has been so low that it is difficult to see him suddenly opening up for a finish. But if Elliott overcommits on a shot and eats a counter, the knockout is always possible.
analysis_sections.betting:
**LEAN — Steve Erceg**

The line on Erceg opened at -250 and has since lengthened all the way to around -196, meaning the market has moved off him — not toward him. That is unusual for a fighter who already won this exact matchup and is fighting on home soil. When a clear favorite drifts like this without reverse line movement confirming sharp action in the other direction, it most often reflects public money that opened the line too short, with the books correcting toward a truer number. Erceg at -196 is a more palatable price than -250, but it still requires roughly 66% implied probability to break even, and with the market tightening the line, the value has compressed.

The honest assessment is that Erceg is the correct side — he is younger by nine years, has already beaten Elliott once by decision, holds the wrestling and reach advantages, and is fighting in Perth. Elliott at 39, with two weight misses in his history and declining pace numbers, needs something dramatically different from him to flip the result. That is not impossible, but nothing in the recent data suggests he has that adjustment in the tank.

However, Erceg's recent output has been genuinely thin. A 1.6 SLpM performance in the first fight and a 0.2 SLpM performance against Osbourne are not numbers that inspire confidence in a dominant, lopsided finish. This fight is likely to look similar to the first — a close-ish decision where Erceg does enough to win without doing much to impress. At -196, you are paying a significant premium for a fighter who may squeak out a win rather than dominate one.

Given the compressed line, the already-won-it-once context, and the lack of prop value in a fight likely to go to scorecards, this sits at a lean rather than a full play. The math on Erceg at -196 leaves roughly 2–3 percentage points of edge depending on your assessed probability — real but thin enough to warrant caution rather than a committed position.

**Stake: 0.5 units on Steve Erceg**
**Target line: -185 or better** — at -196, the value is marginal. If you can find Erceg at -185 or shorter on any book, that is the play. At -200 or longer, pass entirely.

**Data note:** Both fighters have sufficient UFC track records for this analysis to be reliable. The added layer of an already-completed first fight between these two makes the read cleaner than most, though the thin volume numbers on Erceg in recent outings introduce uncertainty about how dominant this performance will look.

**Hedge verdict: NO ARB.** Market data shows only one side of the line pair (Erceg -196 / Elliott +210 implied). Combined implied is approximately 65% + 32% = 97%, which is on the borderline, but without confirmed cross-book pricing this cannot be structured as a guaranteed-profit hedge. Take the lean on Erceg at the best available price.

**Props:** No prop value identified. A fight-goes-to-decision prop would mathematically make sense given both fighters' recent decision-heavy outputs and Erceg's low finishing rate, but without a prop line to de-vig and price against, no recommendation can be made here.

**If you're passing this fight:** The right call. The edge is thin, the fight is a likely replay of the first one, and -196 on a decision-bound flyweight is not a spot where you need to force a position.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Marwan Rahiki -- Style & Profile

- At 23 and unbeaten at 9-0, Rahiki is a high-volume pressure striker who throws at 7.75 significant strikes per minute — one of the busier outputs you'll see at featherweight. His accuracy has climbed across his last three fights (41% → 53% → 55%), meaning the volume is becoming more purposeful, not just busier.
- Every single one of his last three wins ended by KO/TKO, and none of them went past Round 2. He's not grinding people out — he's walking them down and finishing them cleanly, which suggests real power behind the output, not just activity.
- He absorbs 5.90 strikes per minute, which is on the high side. He's willing to trade, and so far he's been the better man in those exchanges, but that absorption rate is a number worth watching as he faces better opposition.
- His grappling is essentially nonexistent in the stats — zero takedown attempts in his UFC run — but he has perfect takedown defense. He's a pure striker who wants to stand and bang, and so far no one has tested that at all.
- The progression fight to fight is real: against Mulumba he was working at 3.19 strikes per minute; against Schmid he was at 7.9 with 55% accuracy. That's not the same fighter the books priced two fights ago.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Ollie Schmid -- Style & Profile

- Schmid's entire UFC profile is, at this point, a single fight — a first-round KO loss to Rahiki. With only one fight worth of UFC data available, almost everything here comes with a significant caveat about sample size.
- What the numbers show from that one fight is bleak: 3.23 strikes landed per minute, 30% accuracy, 45% strike defense, and 7.90 absorbed per minute. Absorbing nearly eight significant strikes a minute against a heavy-handed pressure fighter is a recipe for exactly what happened.
- He has no recorded takedown attempts and zero submission attempts across his UFC career. If his path to winning runs through wrestling or the clinch, there's no statistical evidence it exists — at least not at UFC level.
- His 4-3 overall record includes wins, but the one time he's been put in with a truly active and accurate striker at this level, he was stopped in under three minutes. There's no data to suggest a different outcome in a rematch.
- The circumstances of this being a rematch — and the rematch happening with a 1-day layoff listed for both fighters — are unusual data flags. The fight data itself appears to have been recorded already (the May 2, 2026 result is in both records), which means the betting market here is pricing a fight whose outcome is already known in the system data.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The data here raises an immediate flag that has to be addressed before anything else: both fighters show a 1-day layoff, and both fighters' "last fight" is already recorded as this exact matchup — Rahiki defeating Schmid by KO/TKO in Round 1 at 2:47 on May 2, 2026. The last-3 splits for Schmid consist entirely of this fight. This is not a preview of an upcoming bout — the system data reflects a result that has already been determined.

Setting that aside and treating it as a style analysis exercise: the matchup was always structurally lopsided. Rahiki's 7.75 strikes per minute against Schmid's 45% strike defense meant Schmid was going to eat a lot of clean shots. Schmid's own 30% striking accuracy means he wasn't landing enough back to deter a pressure fighter. When a high-volume striker with genuine stopping power faces someone absorbing nearly eight significant strikes per minute, the finish tends to come quickly — and it did, in under three minutes of the first round.

Schmid's path to victory in any version of this matchup required him to either find a takedown to change the fight's location (he has never attempted one at UFC level), or survive Rahiki's early output and hope for late-fight attrition (Rahiki hasn't needed past Round 2 yet). Neither path materialized, and the data offers no strong evidence either would have.
analysis_sections.endings:

**#1 -- Marwan Rahiki wins by KO/TKO, Round 1**
Likelihood: High
Why: This is what already happened according to the fight data in the system — Rahiki stopped Schmid at 2:47 of Round 1. Structurally, it was always the most likely outcome: Rahiki's volume, accuracy, and stopping power against Schmid's poor strike defense and limited output made an early finish the obvious conclusion.

**#2 -- Marwan Rahiki wins by KO/TKO, Round 2**
Likelihood: Low
Why: If Schmid had survived the first round, Rahiki's pressure was never going to let off. His last three fights all ended in Rounds 1 or 2, and his pace doesn't drop — he'd have finished the job in the second.

**#3 -- Ollie Schmid wins by KO/TKO**
Likelihood: Very Low
Why: Schmid has the height and reach to create some awkwardness, and Rahiki's 5.90 strikes absorbed per minute shows he takes shots. But with 30% accuracy and no established power finish on his record in this context, Schmid landing a game-changing shot against a pressure fighter with perfect takedown defense is a long shot. The fight history says it didn't happen.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Brando Pericic -- Style & Profile

- Pericic is a pure volume striker who throws at a genuinely elite rate — his career average of 11.0 significant strikes per minute is exceptional for heavyweight, and his 57% accuracy tells you those aren't just wild swings. He walks opponents down and keeps throwing until the lights go out.
- All three of his UFC wins have come by KO/TKO, and two of them ended in under two minutes of Round 1. His finishing pace is among the fastest at heavyweight right now — he doesn't grind, he overwhelms.
- His defensive profile is mixed. A 58% strike defense is solid for the division, and a 4.26 SApM suggests he's not standing in front of everything — but against elite power, that absorption rate will matter.
- He has zero UFC takedown attempts, which means he's a pure stand-and-deliver heavyweight. He's not hunting the clinch, not shooting for legs — this is a kickboxing fight every time he steps in the cage.
- At 31, he's in his physical prime for heavyweight, and his last three performances show no signs of slowing. If anything, he looks sharper and more composed than when he entered the UFC.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Shamil Gaziev -- Style & Profile

- Gaziev has been knocked out in back-to-back fights, most recently by Waldo Cortes-Acosta in the first round and then by Pericic himself in Round 2. Before that, he was stopped by Rozenstruik. Three KO/TKO losses in his last five fights, two of them coming in the first two rounds — this is a fighter whose chin has been seriously compromised.
- His strike defense is a glaring liability at 45% career average — less than half of incoming significant strikes are being avoided. He absorbs 4.92 per minute, which is among the highest in the division. When the opponent has power, that combination is dangerous.
- At 36 years old, there's no reason to expect the chin to suddenly become sturdier. Age at heavyweight tends to accelerate the decline of durability rather than reverse it, and Gaziev is already past the cliff.
- His offensive output has been inconsistent. He put together some solid performances earlier in his UFC run, but his last three fights show him operating between 2.86 and 4.39 SLpM with accuracy dropping as low as 40%. He's not producing at a level that threatens elite competition consistently.
- He did attempt a takedown against Pericic in their first meeting (1.72/15min), suggesting he's capable of mixing in the grappling, but with 33% career takedown accuracy, it's not a reliable path to changing the fight's location.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This is a rematch, and the first meeting already told us everything we need to know about the structural problem Gaziev faces here. Pericic is faster, throws more, and hits hard enough to finish heavyweights — all three of his UFC wins ended by KO/TKO, two of them inside two minutes. Against a fighter absorbing nearly five significant strikes per minute with a 45% strike defense, Pericic's output is a near-perfect stylistic weapon. The moment Gaziev tries to stand and trade at range, he's playing directly into the one skillset Pericic has spent his UFC career sharpening.

The one avenue Gaziev could theoretically exploit is a grappling-based gameplan. He has an 83% takedown defense, which is decent, and he did attempt takedowns in the first fight — but with only 33% success rate on his shots historically, and against a fighter in Pericic who hasn't been taken down (71% TD defense), the path to the floor is narrow. Even if Gaziev gets a takedown, his submission average is negligible (0.3 attempts per 15 minutes), and Pericic has shown no vulnerability on the ground in his UFC run. Gaziev doesn't finish fights from top position either — his recent finishes have mostly come from striking, which puts him right back into the exchange problem.

The age gap matters here too. Pericic is five years younger and fighting with upward momentum, while Gaziev is 36 and coming off consecutive knockout losses. The net intangible picture heavily favors Pericic — he's on a three-fight KO streak, he already beat this man, and his opponent is aging into exactly the kind of durability decline that makes rematches against big punchers even more lopsided the second time.
analysis_sections.endings:

**#1 -- Brando Pericic wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: High
Why: Pericic already stopped Gaziev inside two rounds in their first meeting, and nothing about Gaziev's profile suggests the second fight goes differently. His defensive numbers are poor, his chin has been cracked repeatedly, and he's aging into the phase where those issues compound rather than resolve. Pericic throws at an extraordinary rate with 57% accuracy — Gaziev will eat shots early and often, and the finish should come before the final bell.

**#2 -- Brando Pericic wins by KO/TKO, Round 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: If Gaziev survives the early exchanges — perhaps by clinching, pressuring, or getting fortunate with the rhythm — Pericic's output could slow slightly. His last three fights were all short, so his Round 3 cardio in a competitive fight is genuinely untested. However, even if the fight extends, Gaziev's defensive problems don't disappear over time, and Pericic remains the bigger threat with his hands.

**#3 -- Shamil Gaziev wins by KO/TKO**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Gaziev is a legitimate heavyweight who has finished fighters before, and Pericic absorbs 4.26 strikes per minute himself — he's not untouchable. If Gaziev can land something clean early before Pericic gets his rhythm going, there's a puncher's scenario here. It's unlikely given the history and the trajectory, but it cannot be dismissed entirely at heavyweight where one big shot changes everything.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Louie Sutherland -- Style & Profile

- Sutherland's career numbers tell a wrestler's story on paper — nearly 5 takedowns per 15 minutes and 68% striking accuracy — but his recent fights paint a messier picture. He's been KO'd in round one by Pericic and submitted in round one by Walker in back-to-back fights before this rematch with Tuivasa.
- His striking volume has collapsed in his last three outings. Against Tuivasa in May he landed just 0.6 significant strikes per minute; against Pericic he threw more (5.0/min) but walked into a first-round knockout. He is not a fighter who can sustain a striking game at heavyweight pace.
- His 30% strike defense career average is one of the worst numbers you'll see at heavyweight. He absorbs almost everything thrown at him, and the two back-to-back first-round finishes before this rematch confirmed that the chin is under real stress — he has been stopped in R1 twice in his last three fights, once by strikes and once by submission after being beaten up.
- He has two career weight misses on record, which at heavyweight is a red flag for body composition management and long-term cardio reliability.
- His path to winning in the original fight was built almost entirely on grinding wrestling and controlling the pace to nothing — 0.6 strikes landed per minute in a three-round fight is not striking, it is survival. He won that decision by doing just enough to not lose it.
- At 32 with two back-to-back stoppages immediately before this rematch, his confidence and chin are both in a questionable place heading into a fight against the same man who just handed him a blueprint.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Tai Tuivasa -- Style & Profile

- Tuivasa built his name as one of the most dangerous one-punch knockout artists at heavyweight — three consecutive KO wins over Greg Hardy, Augusto Sakai, and Derrick Lewis made him a legitimate contender and a fan favorite. All three came in the first two rounds, and all three were absolutely savage. That version of Tuivasa was real.
- The version we have seen over the last two-plus years is almost unrecognizable. He has lost eight straight fights. Three of those were unanimous or split decisions where he threw almost nothing — 0.4 strikes per minute against Sutherland, 0.13 per minute against Teixeira, 0.6 per minute against Rozenstruik. These are not numbers from a struggling fighter. These are numbers from a fighter who is not competing.
- His career 4.61 significant strikes absorbed per minute is an alarming number, and his 42% strike defense career average means he eats the majority of what gets thrown at him. In his prime that was fine because he could end the fight instantly. Now that his output has cratered, he is absorbing damage without inflicting it.
- He does zero grappling — literally 0.00 takedowns per 15 minutes career average, 0% takedown accuracy. His entire game plan has always been to stand, throw, and finish. When the finishing power is not translating into action, he has no secondary gear to fall back on.
- The 8-fight losing streak covers every scenario: knockout losses, submission losses, and decision losses where he barely threw a punch. That range of outcomes suggests the problem is not just physical — his engagement level has fundamentally changed from the hungry knockout artist he was at 28.
- At 33, his southpaw stance theoretically provides a structural wrinkle against orthodox fighters, but that advantage only matters if he is willing to engage and use distance and angles — something his last three fights show he is not doing.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

We already have a result from this exact matchup: Sutherland won a unanimous decision on May 2, 2026, the same card this fight appears to be from. The data in front of us is the rematch. That changes the analytical lens considerably — the question is not "how does this matchup play out" but "does Tuivasa have any reason to produce a different result this time?"

Sutherland's path to the first decision was built on wrestling. He averaged 1.0 takedown per 15 minutes in that fight against a man who defends takedowns at 58% — which is mediocre at heavyweight. Tuivasa's only real answer to a wrestling-heavy opponent is his knockout power, and in the first fight he managed just 0.4 strikes per minute. His KO threat requires him to actually throw punches, and in that fight he did not. Sutherland is not a power striker — he absorbed 2.03 significant strikes per minute over his career but his two stoppages came from fighters with genuine finishing credentials. Tuivasa's recent form shows he can no longer generate the volume or aggression needed to land that fight-ending shot against a grinding wrestling game.

The net intangible picture is not clean for either man. Sutherland has back-to-back round-one finishes before this rematch — he has been stopped twice, once by strikes and once by a submission after getting broken down. His chin and confidence are both legitimate concerns. But Tuivasa is on an 8-fight slide with vanishingly small output, and the first meeting between these two already gave Sutherland the blueprint. The market moving sharply toward Tuivasa despite him losing the first fight and losing eight in a row is puzzling, and while that line movement is informative, it has to be weighed against the documented evidence that Tuivasa simply does not engage anymore. Sutherland's wrestling and the extreme pace suppression he imposes represent the exact stylistic problem Tuivasa has shown no ability to solve.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Sutherland wins by unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: High
Why: The first fight between these two ended exactly this way, with Sutherland controlling pace and grinding out rounds with minimal output from either fighter. Tuivasa's last three fights all went to decision with almost no striking volume on his end — he generated 0.13 strikes per minute against Teixeira, 0.4 against Sutherland, and 0.6 against Rozenstruik. If Sutherland can keep repeating the grind-and-survive formula he used in the first fight, the result is the same. Probability approximately 40%.

**#2 -- Tuivasa wins by KO/TKO, Rounds 1-2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Sutherland has been stopped in round one in two of his last three fights, and his 30% strike defense career average means he gets tagged cleanly. If Tuivasa comes out with the urgency and aggression he showed against Sakai and Derrick Lewis — which the sharp line move to -150 may be hinting at — and connects early, Sutherland's chin is fragile enough to fold. The question is whether Tuivasa can generate that level of output anymore. His last three fights suggest he cannot, but the rematch motivation and a home continent crowd may change his engagement level. Probability approximately 25%.

**#3 -- Sutherland wins by KO/TKO or TKO stoppage, Rounds 1-2**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Sutherland was KO'd in round one by Pericic after landing 5.0 strikes per minute — his one fight where he opened up. His career output is low and he doesn't carry significant power at heavyweight. The only path here is Tuivasa getting sloppy, eating repeated strikes, and getting stopped — which given Tuivasa's passivity in recent fights is not impossible if Sutherland is the aggressor. But Sutherland has never finished a fight in his last three, so this is a low-probability scenario. Probability approximately 15%.

**#4 -- Tuivasa wins by decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: In the original fight Sutherland won and Tuivasa barely threw a punch, so this would require Tuivasa to outwork Sutherland over three rounds with a better wrestling answer and more striking output than he showed the first time. Given his last eight fights and zero grappling output, it's the hardest result to construct a credible argument for. Probability approximately 10%.

## Cam Rowston -- Style & Profile

- Rowston is a pressure striker who comes forward and hunts stoppages. Three of his last five UFC wins came by KO/TKO, and all three stoppages happened inside two rounds — he puts the gas down early and makes opponents pay for standing in front of him.
- His career striking rate of 4.60 significant strikes per minute is strong for the middleweight division, but the last three fights show a noticeable drop to 1.6–4.47 per minute depending on the opponent. Against Bryczek in the first meeting, he was conservative at just 1.6 SLpM — likely a symptom of gameplan-first fighting once he established control.
- His defensive grappling is his clearest liability. A 33% takedown defense rate is well below average, meaning anyone with credible wrestling can slow him down or neutralize him. That said, recent opponents haven't tested him much there.
- His striking accuracy sits around 48% for the career and dipped to the low 30s against Petroski and Brundage — suggesting he gets a bit wild in brawls but still finds the finish. He doesn't need to be clean to be effective.
- At 31 with a 15-3 record, Rowston is ascending. His three most recent fights are all wins over UFC-caliber competition, and he finished two of them in the first two rounds. He enters this rematch as the fighter with momentum on his side and with the advantage of knowing exactly what Bryczek does.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Robert Bryczek -- Style & Profile

- Bryczek is primarily a defensive-minded fighter who struggles to generate consistent offensive output. His career average of 2.80 significant strikes per minute is modest, and in two of his last three fights he landed at just 0.53 and 0.80 SLpM — barely registering on offense. The Brad Tavares fight (3.33 SLpM, KO finish) looks like an outlier, not a trend.
- The volume he absorbs is the most alarming number in his profile: 4.51 significant strikes absorbed per minute for his UFC career. That is a very high number for a middleweight, and it explains why his finishes often come from exchanges rather than sustained pressure — he gets hit a lot before he lands.
- At 35, he is past the age where most middleweights can absorb sustained punishment without degradation. The combination of high strike absorption across his UFC career and his age creates a meaningful durability concern heading into a rematch with someone who already beat him by decision.
- His lone recent bright spot — the Tavares finish — came in September 2025, and he hasn't looked dangerous outside of that. Against Rowston the first time and against Potieria, he was largely inactive (0.53 and 0.80 SLpM) and lost both times by unanimous decision.
- His takedown defense is listed at 71% and his takedown accuracy at 100%, but both numbers are built on extremely thin sample sizes. He averages just 0.36 takedowns per 15 minutes, meaning he almost never shoots. The grappling numbers are statistical noise more than genuine tendencies.
- The line movement tells a story here: Bryczek opened as a significant favorite (around -163) but has drifted substantially to +250 as the market reassessed. That kind of movement on a fighter who just fought this opponent a week ago suggests the market is pricing in what the tape already shows.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

We already have a direct result to anchor this rematch: Rowston beat Bryczek by unanimous decision three days ago. That result isn't noise — it's evidence. Rowston out-landed Bryczek badly over three rounds, limiting him to just 0.53 significant strikes per minute while keeping his own offense ticking. This is a rematch booked almost immediately after the first fight, which makes the first fight the single most relevant data point in the analysis.

Stylistically, Rowston's pressure and accuracy create a real problem for Bryczek, who relies on counter-striking but gets hit far too often to thrive in that role against a disciplined aggressor. Bryczek's 4.51 SLpM absorbed career average indicates he routinely walks into punches, and Rowston — who has five KO/TKO finishes on his record — is the type to find the finish when an opponent starts getting hit. The reach disadvantage for Bryczek (78" vs. 75") means Rowston can operate on the outside and dictate range, which compounds the problem for a fighter who needs to close distance to land.

The only realistic path for Bryczek runs through one scenario: a counter shot lands clean early, similar to how he stopped Tavares. He does have finishing power — the Tavares KO in round three proves it. But Rowston has already seen Bryczek's offense for fifteen minutes and held him to a fraction of his average output. Adjusting for a second fight, Rowston is more prepared and Bryczek hasn't shown the offensive consistency to suggest he can reverse a clear result on two days' rest against the same opponent.

If Rowston fights conservatively and controls distance, three rounds of accumulated work gives him the decision again.

Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Rowston has finished three of his last five UFC opponents by stoppage, two of them inside two rounds. Bryczek absorbs strikes at a very high rate for a middleweight, and at 35 against a power striker he's already lost to, early stoppages are a real possibility. If Rowston is aggressive from the opening bell rather than measured, he may not need all three rounds.

Likelihood: Low
Why: Bryczek demonstrated he can finish when the timing is right, as the Tavares knockout showed. If he times a counter early and Rowston walks in recklessly, the fight could swing in an instant. This is the only realistic path for Bryczek — one clean shot before Rowston settles into his rhythm.

## Junior Tafa -- Style & Profile

- Tafa is a heavy-handed power striker who ends fights quickly when he's on. Three of his last four wins have come by KO/TKO, and the two most recent ones — Parker Porter in 1:24 and Kevin Christian in 2:42 — were first-round stoppages. When opponents give him space and stand in front of him, he's dangerous.
- His striking output surged dramatically in the most recent fight: 7.78 significant strikes per minute at 67.7% accuracy against Christian, compared to a career average of 3.62 per minute. That's not a trend built over time — it's one fight — but it tells you what happens when the matchup suits him.
- The soft underbelly of his game is the ground. He has been submitted four times in his UFC career, including losses to Tuco Tokkos and Billy Elekana in back-to-back fights leading up to the Christian rematch. His submission defense simply doesn't exist at a UFC level. He has recorded zero takedown attempts and zero submission attempts across his entire UFC run.
- He also has a KO loss on his record (Marcos Rogerio de Lima, R2) and has been stopped five times overall in his UFC career (4 subs, 1 KO). Once the fight leaves striking range, his win probability collapses rapidly.
- At 29 and fighting at 205 lbs on short turnover, there are no obvious physical decline concerns here — this is a fighter at the peak of his athletic window.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Jacob Malkoun -- Style & Profile

- Malkoun is a pressure-forward wrestler who wins by controlling cage real estate and grinding out decisions — six of his last nine results have gone to the scorecards, and he's won five of them. He's not a flashy finisher; he wins by accumulating advantages across three rounds.
- His takedown output is elite for the division at 5.39 per 15 minutes, though his 42% accuracy means he's shooting frequently and relying on volume to get opponents to the mat. Against anyone who can sprawl consistently, that becomes a tiring exercise.
- His striking accuracy of 56% is genuinely good — well above UFC average — but his recent output tells a different story. In his last fight (this exact rematch), he threw barely anything, landing at 0.4 SLpM with 50% accuracy on a tiny sample. He won that fight by doing just enough. Against Petroski he had genuine power (KO in R2), but that looks like an outlier against mostly decision wins.
- Defensively, his 55% strike defense is middling, and he absorbed only 2.33 strikes per minute across his career — manageable, but he has been knocked out cold. Phil Hawes finished him in 18 seconds in 2022, a first-round flash that flags his vulnerability to fast, sharp starters.
- At 30, he's in his athletic prime with no age-related concerns. He's also fought recently — the layoff listed here is effectively zero because this is a rematch of a fight that just happened, and his camp would have been fully prepared for this specific opponent.
- His path in this rematch is the same as the first fight: wrestle early, control position, stay patient, win on the cards. He's done it once against this man already.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Gerald Meerschaert -- Style & Profile

- Meerschaert is a submission specialist — his three most recent wins all came by submission, and 1.5 submission attempts per 15 minutes is above the UFC middleweight average. But he has to get the fight to the ground first, and his 29% takedown accuracy and 40% takedown defense make that an increasingly difficult ask.
- He has lost five straight, and the manner of those losses is alarming. He's been knocked out (Oleksiejczuk, Joe Pyfer), submitted (Daukaus, de Ridder), and outpointed (Tavares, Malkoun). The striking losses are the most concerning — absorbing 3.44 significant strikes per minute while defending at only 53% means he's getting hit cleanly and regularly.
- In his last three fights, his striking output essentially collapsed to near-zero. Against Daukaus he landed nothing at all. Against Oleksiejczuk he was generating less than 1 SLpM before getting stopped. Against Malkoun in their first meeting he managed 0.27 SLpM. He is not setting the pace in any of these fights, and that passivity is getting him finished or outscored.
- At 38 years old in a physically demanding weight class, the decline signals are stacking up fast. He's being finished by opponents who aren't considered elite strikers — Oleksiejczuk is a solid hitter, not a knockout artist in the Adesanya tier — and his defensive reflexes look noticeably slower on tape.
- His southpaw stance and long reach (77 inches) are legitimate tools — he creates angles orthodox fighters haven't seen as frequently — but those advantages only matter if he can get into range and create scrambles. Against a wrestler like Malkoun who controls distance on his own terms, the stance edge is limited.
- One weight miss in his history isn't disqualifying, but at 38 with a body that's clearly struggled to perform at the level required against current middleweight depth, his best days are behind him and the data makes that undeniable.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The fundamental problem for Meerschaert in this rematch is the same one that beat him in the first fight: Malkoun takes him down, holds position, and Meerschaert's offensive grappling never gets a clean entry. Meerschaert's entire dangerous game runs through submissions — and submissions require getting to the mat on your own terms, not being put there by someone else. His 40% takedown defense is the single most important number in this matchup. Malkoun shoots 5+ times per 15 minutes and connects on 42% of those attempts. The math says Malkoun puts him on the mat multiple times per round, and once there, Meerschaert's guard and bottom-game are fine but not special enough to reverse position against a 30-year-old pressure wrestler. The rematch data point — 0.0 takedowns for Meerschaert in the first fight — tells you how that dynamic actually played out.

Meerschaert's only realistic path involves keeping the fight standing long enough to either land his southpaw left hand on a charging Malkoun or to create a scramble where Malkoun's guard gets exposed. Malkoun has been knocked out before (Hawes), so the power threat is not fictional. But Malkoun at 30 is not the same fighter Hawes caught in 18 seconds — he's four years more experienced, and he's already lived through a full fight with Meerschaert and knows exactly how to navigate the submission danger. The five-loss slide, the near-zero striking output in recent fights, and the age gap make Meerschaert's window for an upset very narrow.

The net intangible picture heavily favors Malkoun. He's 30, active, coming off a win in this exact matchup, and he has a completed camp tailored to this opponent. Meerschaert is 38, on a five-fight losing streak, and is being asked to solve a puzzle he couldn't solve 90 days ago. The line moving from -600 all the way to -1800 on Malkoun reflects the market fully pricing in a rematch where the stylistic reading is near-unanimous — Malkoun controls this fight the same way he controlled the first one.

Meerschaert's 40% takedown defense and near-zero striking output in their first meeting suggests this pattern repeats cleanly.

**#2 -- Jacob Malkoun wins by TKO/KO, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Meerschaert has been finished in the first round in two of his last five fights, and his striking defense (53% career) combined with an observable slowdown in defensive reflexes at 38 makes him hittable. If Malkoun decides to box more aggressively than he did in fight one, or if a takedown leads to ground-and-pound before Meerschaert can recover his guard, a stoppage becomes real. His Petroski KO shows Malkoun has finishing power when the opportunity opens up.

**#3 -- Gerald Meerschaert wins by submission, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Meerschaert's submission game is legitimate — three submission wins in recent memory — and if Malkoun gets sloppy off a failed takedown or dives into a poor position, the neck is genuinely in danger. Malkoun's 0.8 submission attempts per 15 minutes suggests he's not a grappler who tries to finish, and that calculated approach usually keeps him safe, but it's the one scenario where Meerschaert wins this rematch.

The problem is the price. Malkoun has moved from -600 to -1800. At -1800, you are risking $1,800 to win $100. To justify that bet, Malkoun would need to win this fight approximately 95% of the time. **On Meerschaert +450:** At +450, the implied probability is roughly 18.2%. If Malkoun genuinely wins 80-82% of the time, Meerschaert wins 18-20% — which is almost exactly at fair value on a de-vigged basis. There's no meaningful edge on the dog either.

**Target line for Malkoun if value existed:** The bet only becomes interesting mathematically somewhere around -350 to -400, which the market left behind weeks ago.

**Market read:** The line movement from -600 to -1800 is an enormous directional move, entirely consistent with the public hammering an obvious rematch favorite with a sympathetic narrative (five-fight loser takes a rematch). This is not reverse line movement — it's mass public action on Malkoun. That confirms what the stats already say: Malkoun wins. It does not create value.

**Hedge verdict: NO ARB.** The combined implied probability on this fight is well above 100%. There is no arbitrage opportunity.

**Props worth considering:** If a "fight goes to decision" prop is available, it deserves a close look. The first fight went to a decision, Malkoun's win methods lean heavily on decisions (5 of his last 9 fights), and Meerschaert — despite his losses — has gone the distance multiple times. If that prop is available around -150 or better on "yes, goes to decision," it likely has value given the base rates and how the first fight played out. De-vig before placing; prop vig in this market is typically 10%+, so the target should be around -130 or longer to clear the bar.

**Final word:** This is a fight where the read is easy and the bet is impossible. Malkoun wins. The number doesn't let you profit from being right. Pass.

Why: Meerschaert has been finished in the first round in two of his last five fights, and his striking defense (53% career) combined with an observable slowdown in defensive reflexes at 38 makes him hittable. If Malkoun decides to box more aggressively than he did in fight one, or if a takedown leads to ground-and-pound before Meerschaert can recover his guard, a stoppage becomes real. His Petroski KO shows Malkoun has finishing power when the opportunity opens up.

**#3 -- Gerald Meerschaert wins by submission, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Meerschaert's submission game is legitimate — three submission wins in recent memory — and if Malkoun gets sloppy off a failed takedown or dives into a poor position, the neck is genuinely in danger. <!--BETTING-->
**Tier: NO BET (on the moneyline)**

Malkoun is the clear and correct pick in this rematch — he won the first fight convincingly, the stylistic edge is real, and Meerschaert's five-fight losing streak makes the narrative straightforward. There is zero disagreement here about who the better fighter is in this matchup.

The problem is the price. Malkoun has moved from -600 to -1800. At -1800, you are risking $1,800 to win $100. To justify that bet, Malkoun would need to win this fight approximately 95% of the time. Even accounting for all the edges above — age, recency, style, the first-fight result — MMA's inherent variance (rogue submission, one-shot KO from a southpaw left hand) makes 95% an indefensible number. The line reflects a public/sharp pile-on on an obvious favorite, not a mispriced market. There is no value here.

**Primary Pick: PASS on Malkoun -1800**
Confidence in Malkoun winning: approximately 80–82%. The line requires 94.7%. That gap is the problem — the market is pricing this correctly, perhaps even slightly over-correcting for the rematch factor.

**On Meerschaert +450:** At +450, the implied probability is roughly 18.2%. If Malkoun genuinely wins 80-82% of the time, Meerschaert wins 18-20% — which is almost exactly at fair value on a de-vigged basis. There's no meaningful edge on the dog either.

**Target line for Malkoun if value existed:** The bet only becomes interesting mathematically somewhere around -350 to -400, which the market left behind weeks ago.

**Market read:** The line movement from -600 to -1800 is an enormous directional move, entirely consistent with the public hammering an obvious rematch favorite with a sympathetic narrative (five-fight loser takes a rematch). This is not reverse line movement — it's mass public action on Malkoun. That confirms what the stats already say: Malkoun wins. It does not create value.
